text: This is a new technique to repair an incisional hernia. This is a sixty-three year old female who had had a partial colectomy period. This was done in conjunction with a parasophageal hernia repair and at the end of the parasophageal hernia repair a paramedian rectorectus dissection was performed. Starting about two-thirds of the way between the medial and lateral border of the rectus sheath, the posterior rectus space is entered. This allows for safe dissection without disturbing the neurovascular bundles to the rectus muscle. This is a similar position to a TARS dissection The key to this dissection is making sure that you are in the right space. A preoperative CT is very beneficial. Some patients do have anatomy that is favorable to see in the rectus muscle through the posterior fascia. An ultrasound intraoperatively could also be used. After dissecting carefully the muscle off of the posterior rectus sheath, the area of the hernia is approached. Careful dissection when you approach the hernia sac is important and coordination with your surgical assistant is very crucial to ensure that you do not enter the skin where it is close to the hernia sac.

The surgeon carefully dissects the anterior rectus fascia, separating it from the posterior rectus fascia. The dissection proceeds along the opposite side to clearly identify the rectus sheath in the midline area.

The surgeon reduces both hernia sacs, carefully separating them from the subcutaneous tissue. Minimal cautery is applied near the skin to avoid burns from the intra-abdominal approach. In the midline, the rectus sheath is challenging to identify, but once the rectus muscle is located, the anterior fascia is dissected from the posterior fascia, and the retro-rectus space is accessed on the opposite side. The anterior sheath is clearly delineated, and the hernia defect is released.

The surgeon places a figure-of-eight number one PDS suture in three locations to approximate the anterior rectus fascia, ensuring closure without tension. The suture is tied using a square knot converted to a slip knot, allowing for adequate tension and secure approximation of the tissue.

The surgeon uses three interrupted number one PDS sutures to approximate the anterior fascia, followed by a number one Stratifex suture to close the midline completely. The suture is visibly long, requiring careful management for efficient closure.

The surgeon prepares the space for adequate dissection and closure of the fascia, reducing pressure to eight millimeters of mercury before approximating the anterior rectus sheath. A phase XST mesh, twenty by ten centimeters and shaped into an oval, is positioned with the non-adherent side facing the intra-abdominal area. A 2-0 V-lock suture is used to approximate the tissue to the fascia, carefully placing it circumferentially.

The surgeon places a mesh in the retrorectus space and secures it circumferentially, aligning it with the anterior midline fascia. A drain is inserted into the posterior rectus space through a lateral port site, extending into the posterior rectus sheath. The posterior sheath is closed using a running zero V-lock suture in the paramedian, ensuring the mesh is excluded from the intra-abdominal cavity to promote ingrowth. Hernia sacs remain intact without pexing.
